AI Platform Modernization · 53-Slide Reference Template

Reference template v1.0
53 slides · 16:9
Microsoft·AI Platform Modernization
The platform decision behind every production AI outcome.
A strategic and technical brief on why enterprise AI initiatives stall before production, and the reference architecture that closes the gap between pilot and scale.
Azure AI Foundry GitHub Copilot Enterprise Azure Kubernetes Service Agentic DevOps
Frontier Cockpit TeamSoftware Global Black Belt
Strategic & Technical BriefClient engagement · 2026
Confidential · Client brief 01 / 53
Session context
A working session to decide the platform scope and the first phase.
Client
Contoso FinancialPlatform & Engineering leadership
Date
2026-05-2090-minute briefing
Session type
Strategic & technical briefDecision-oriented, not a pitch
In the room
CTO, Head of PlatformSecurity and SRE leads
Outcome we are after: agreement on a three-week assessment window and the two candidate workloads to baseline. Placeholders for the reference template.
Microsoft·Front matter
02·53
Agenda

Six sections, from where the market is to what to do next.

This brief follows a single line: diagnose the gap, reframe the solution as a platform, then lay out the architecture, plan, and investment to get there.
01

Context

Slides 05–12
  • Market adoption signals
  • Landscape of approaches
  • Where teams sit today
  • A client's starting point
02

The problem

Slides 13–21
  • Cost of delay
  • Three root causes
  • The broken path
  • What compounds it
03

The solution

Slides 22–30
  • The reference stack
  • Operating principles
  • The governed path
  • Objections answered
04

Architecture

Slides 31–38
  • Component diagram
  • Runtime layer detail
  • CI/CD with gates
  • Build options
05

The plan

Slides 39–44
  • Modernization roadmap
  • Assessment week by week
  • The engagement team
  • Ownership and risks
06

Investment

Slides 45–48
  • Engagement sizes
  • The ask
  • The recommendation
Navigation · Section dividers mark each transition. The deck is built to be read in order or sampled by section.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Front matter
03·53
Executive summary

Three things to take from this brief, before the detail.

If the room reads only one slide, it is this one. Each column is a claim, the evidence behind it, and what it means for the decision ahead.
1

The blocker is the platform, not the model

Pilots reach a working demo and stall. The recurring cause is the absence of a shared foundation: environments, data access, and a route to production.

So what · Solve it once at the platform layer, not repeatedly per project.
2

An opinionated stack closes the gap

Four layers, each owned once, remove the friction line-items teams hit today. Teams consume the platform; they do not re-derive it.

So what · The reference architecture is the deliverable, not a slideware idea.
3

Start small, decide on evidence

A three-week assessment produces the client's own baseline. Every commitment after that is a decision made with real numbers.

So what · Low-commitment first step. The roadmap earns the next phase.
Read order · This slide stands alone. The six sections expand each column with evidence and architecture.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
“
The hard part of enterprise AI was never the model. It is the distance between a notebook and a service the business can actually depend on.
Framing position · reference template, illustrative attribution
I
Section 1 · Context
Where the market is on production AI
Adoption is wide and shallow. Pilots are everywhere; production is rare. This section sizes that gap before naming its causes.
Questions this act answers
Q1How wide is AI adoption, really?
Q2How narrow is production?
Q3Where does the typical team sit?
Microsoft·Section 1 · Context
06·53
Section intro

Where the market is, in one honest read.

A section intro is lighter than a divider. It sets up the sub-topic and frames what the next few slides will show, without the full act ceremony.
What this section covers
Adoption is wide. Production is narrow.
Most enterprises now run AI pilots. Far fewer run AI in production. This section sizes that gap with the client's peers as the reference point, so the problem is concrete before we name its causes.
In this section
4 slides, ending on a client snapshot
Key question
How big is the pilot-to-production gap?
Leaves you with
A measured starting point, not an opinion
Pattern note · Use a section intro after a divider when the section is long enough to need its own framing.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 1 · Context
07·53
Adoption signal

Almost everyone is piloting. Almost no one is shipping.

The headline gap is not interest in AI. It is the distance between a team that runs experiments and a team that runs services.
The core metric
9/10
enterprises in this scenario run at least one AI pilot. Illustrative figure.
Placeholder figure · cite real source in production decks
Reach a demo
~80%
of pilots produce a working demonstration of value.
Reach production
~12%
of those demos become a governed, monitored service.
The drop
1
platform layer separates the two numbers above.
Note · All figures are placeholders. Real client decks must cite named, dated sources.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 1 · Context
08·53
Target-state metrics

Six signals that tell you the platform is actually working.

A metrics dashboard mixes tile sizes so the eye lands on what matters. The featured tile carries the headline; the rest give context around it.
Headline signal
2wks
median time to provision a new AI workload, down from nine
Time to production
−70%
pilot to live service
Policy rework
−1stage
governance shifted left
Workloads on platform
14
across six teams
Shared observability
100%
one pane, all teams
Dashboard note · All figures are placeholders. Real dashboards read against the Phase 1 baseline.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 1 · Context
09·53
The landscape

Four ways teams approach AI infrastructure today, and what each costs.

A side-by-side of the common patterns. Most teams are somewhere in the first two rows, and that is the problem.
ApproachWho does itWhat it optimizes forRecursMaturity
Per-project setupEach app teamSpeed of the first demoPer projectLow
Shared scriptsA platform championReuse, informallyPer teamPartial
Cloud-managed PaaSVendorTime to first deployPer workloadMedium
Reference platformOwned once, consumed by allRepeatable path to productionOnceHigh
Read this as

Maturity rises only when the platform is owned once instead of rebuilt per project.

Synthesis line · reference template
Maturity banding is qualitative. Replace with the client's own assessment.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 1 · Context
10·53
Where teams sit today

Most AI work clusters in the wrong quadrant: high ambition, low readiness.

A simple read of the field. Vertical axis is platform maturity; horizontal is AI ambition. The gap is the distance teams must close.
Platform maturity →
Top left

Over-built

Strong platform, little AI demand on it yet.

Top right · target

Production-ready

Platform maturity matches AI ambition.

Bottom left

Early days

Low on both axes. Honest starting point.

Bottom right · where most sit

Ambition outruns foundation

High AI ambition, thin platform.

AI ambition →
Typical client today Peer benchmark
Axis note · Replace dot positions with the client's own self-assessment scores.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 1 · Context
11·53
Expectation vs reality

What teams expect from an AI pilot, and what they get.

The gap between the two columns is not failure. It is the predictable result of building without a platform.
Expectation · going in
  • A pilot proves value in weeks.
  • A successful demo ships to production.
  • The next pilot reuses the first one's work.
  • Governance is a final checkbox.
  • Cost and behavior are visible by default.
→
Reality · without a platform
  • The demo works; the path to production does not.
  • The demo stalls waiting for infrastructure.
  • The next pilot starts from zero again.
  • Governance is a late, expensive surprise.
  • Cost and behavior live in scattered places.
Demos that ship
~12%
reach production
Setup, repeated
Per pilot
not once
Gap
Predictable
and fixable
Outcome figures are directional placeholders. Validate against a client baseline.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 1 · Context
12·53
Case study

One client, same shape of problem, measured before and after.

A case study makes the abstract concrete. Problem, approach, result, in three rows, with the headline outcome pulled into the hero panel.
Reference client
Mid-size financial services firm
~600 engineers · existing Azure footprint · 12 AI pilots, 1 in production
9 → 2
weeks to provision a new AI workload, after the platform
Problem
Every pilot rebuilt its own environment and data access. Eleven of twelve never got a production target.
Approach
A six-week foundation phase: AKS landing zone, standard pipeline, and one pilot migrated onto golden paths.
Result
Provisioning dropped from nine weeks to two. Three further workloads reached production within the next quarter.
Anonymization note · Figures and client descriptor are illustrative. Real case studies use cited, approved data.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 1 · Context
13·53
Stakeholder map

Who to engage closely, and who to keep informed.

A stakeholder map plots influence against interest. The top-right quadrant is where the engagement spends its attention; the rest get the right, lighter touch.
Influence →
High influence · low interest
CFOProcurement
High influence · high interest · engage closely
CTOHead of platformSecurity lead
Low influence · low interest · monitor
Adjacent BUs
Low influence · high interest · keep informed
App teamsSREData engineering
Interest →
Map note · Placement is illustrative. Build the real map with the client sponsor early in Phase 1.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
II
Section 2 · The problem
Why pilots stall before production
The blocker is rarely the model. It is everything underneath: environments, data access, governance, and the route to a running service.
Questions this act answers
Q4Where exactly do pilots break down?
Q5What does the delay cost?
Q6Is this a tooling problem or a platform problem?
Microsoft·Section 2 · The problem
15·53
The gap

Most teams reach a working demo. Far fewer reach a governed service.

The distance between those two states is the platform gap. It shows up as repeated setup, inconsistent data access, and no standard route to production.
The core metric
68%
of AI pilots in this scenario never get a production deployment target assigned.
Placeholder figure · cite real source in production decks
Symptom 1 · Environments
~40h
spent per team, per quarter, re-creating environments by hand.
Symptom 2 · Governance
0
consistent policy gates between an experiment and an endpoint.
Root cause
1
missing platform layer. Every symptom traces back to it.
Note · All figures are placeholders for the reference template.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 2 · The problem
16·53
Cost of delay

The platform gap has a line-item cost. Here is where it accrues.

Five recurring friction points, the team they tax, and the effect on delivery.
Friction pointPrimary ownerEffect on deliveryRecursSeverity
Manual environment setupPlatform teamEach new workload restarts from zero infrastructure.Per projectHigh
Inconsistent data accessData engineeringCredentials and patterns differ across every team.Per teamHigh
No standard CI pathApp teamsEach repo invents its own build and release flow.Per repoMedium
Governance applied lateSecurityPolicy checks happen after build, forcing rework.Per releaseMedium
Fragmented observabilitySRENo shared view of cost, latency, or model behavior.ContinuousWatch
Read this as

Every row is solvable once at the platform layer instead of repeatedly at the project layer.

Synthesis line · reference template
Severity banding is illustrative. Replace with the client's own incident data.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 2 · The problem
17·53
Root causes

Five symptoms, three root causes.

The cost-of-delay table lists what teams feel. These are the three underlying causes that produce all of it.
01

No shared foundation

Every workload re-derives networking, identity, and a release path because nothing provides them by default.

02

Governance bolted on

Policy lives outside the delivery path, so it is discovered late and applied as rework rather than as a property of shipping.

03

No common signal

Cost, latency, and model behavior live in team-local dashboards, so problems surface in weeks instead of hours.

Cause note · Three is deliberate. A long list of causes is a list nobody acts on.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 2 · The problem
18·53
The broken path

Without a platform, the path to production breaks at every hand-off.

Four stages, four points of friction. Each arrow is where a workload waits or stalls.
1

Scaffold

Starts from an empty repo. Infrastructure negotiated by hand.

→
2

Build

Each team invents its own CI. No shared evaluation or policy.

→
3

Deploy

No standard runtime target. Networking and scaling re-decided.

→
4

Operate

Observability is team-local. Problems surface late.

Flow note · Compare this to the governed path in the next section, stage for stage.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 2 · The problem
19·53
What compounds it

The problem does not sit still. Four things make it worse over time.

Left alone, the platform gap compounds. Each row is a force that widens it.
Risk Likelihood Impact Mitigation
Each new pilot adds debt High High Every project built the old way is one more thing to migrate later.
Teams diverge further High Medium Without a shared path, each team's setup drifts further from every other.
Governance debt accrues Medium High Late policy means rework piles up release after release.
Knowledge stays siloed Medium Medium What one team learns the hard way, the next team learns again.
Why this matters now

The gap is cheapest to close today. Every quarter of delay adds workloads to migrate and divergence to undo.

Synthesis line · reference template
Banding note · Likelihood and impact are qualitative. Calibrate with the client.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
“
We did not have an AI problem. We had twelve different definitions of what it meant to put a model in production.
Practitioner voice · reference template, illustrative attribution
Microsoft·Section 2 · The problem
21·53
Problem recap

The problem in three lines, before we turn to the solution.

A mid-deck recap. If the room lost the thread, this is where it picks back up.
1

It is a platform problem

Not a model problem, not a talent problem. The blocker is the absence of a shared foundation.

So what · The fix is structural, not another pilot.
2

It has a real cost

Repeated setup, late governance, fragmented signal. Measurable, and paid every quarter.

So what · Inaction is not free; it compounds.
3

It compounds

Every quarter of delay adds workloads to migrate and divergence to undo.

So what · The cheapest time to act is now.
Recap note · This mirrors the front-matter exec summary, refocused on the problem.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 2 · The problem
22·53

Where the market is, in one honest read.

Adoption is wide. Production is narrow.
Most enterprises now run AI pilots. Far fewer run AI in production. This section sizes that gap with the client's peers as the reference point, so the problem is concrete before we name its causes.

Key question
How big is the pilot-to-production gap?
Leaves you with

Microsoft · Strategic Brief
III
Section 3 · The solution
Platform as the unit of delivery
Stop shipping AI projects. Start shipping a platform that makes the next AI project boring, fast, and governed by default.
Questions this act answers
Q8What does the reference stack look like?
Q9What changes for the teams using it?
Q10What about the obvious objections?
Microsoft·Section 3 · The solution
24·53
Section intro

Where the market is, in one honest read.

A section intro is lighter than a divider. It sets up the sub-topic and frames what the next few slides will show, without the full act ceremony.
What this section covers
Adoption is wide. Production is narrow.
Most enterprises now run AI pilots. Far fewer run AI in production. This section sizes that gap with the client's peers as the reference point, so the problem is concrete before we name its causes.
In this section
4 slides, ending on a client snapshot
Key question
How big is the pilot-to-production gap?
Leaves you with
A measured starting point, not an opinion
Pattern note · Use a section intro after a divider when the section is long enough to need its own framing.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 3 · The solution
25·53
Reference stack

Four layers, each owned once, each removing a row from the cost-of-delay table.

The stack is opinionated on purpose. Teams consume it; they do not re-derive it. Layer colors map to the Microsoft palette.
Experience

Developer portal & golden paths

Self-service templates so a new AI workload starts from a governed baseline, not a blank repo.

Delivery

Standard CI/CD with policy gates

One build-and-release path. Governance runs inside the pipeline, not after it.

Runtime

Azure Kubernetes Service landing zone

A consistent place to run workloads, with networking, identity, and scaling decided once.

Intelligence

Azure AI Foundry & model governance

Shared model catalog, evaluation, and observability so every team sees cost and behavior the same way.

Architecture note · Layer names are generic. Real decks name the client's specific services and versions.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 3 · The solution
26·53
Operating principles

The stack works because three principles hold it together.

Architecture describes the what. These principles describe the how, and they keep the platform from drifting back into per-project chaos.
01

Opinionated by default

The platform makes the common choices so teams do not. A new workload inherits networking, identity, and a release path.

02

Governed in the path

Policy runs inside the pipeline, not as a gate discovered late. Compliance becomes a property of shipping.

03

Observable end to end

One shared view of cost, latency, and model behavior. Every team reads the same dashboard.

Principle note · These are deliberately few. A long list of principles is a list nobody follows.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 3 · The solution
27·53
Before / after

What changes for the teams consuming the platform.

The platform layer is invisible when it works. The visible result is a different day-to-day for app teams, security, and SRE.
Before · project-by-project
  • New workload starts from an empty repository and a wiki page.
  • Each team negotiates its own data access and secrets.
  • Security review is a gate discovered late, near release.
  • Cost and latency live in scattered, team-local dashboards.
  • Production readiness is argued case by case.
→
After · platform-as-default
  • New workload starts from a golden-path template.
  • Data access patterns are inherited, not re-negotiated.
  • Policy gates run inside the standard pipeline.
  • One shared view of cost, latency, and model behavior.
  • Production readiness is a checklist the platform enforces.
Setup time
−70%
environment provisioning
Policy rework
−1 stage
governance shifts left
Time to prod
Weeks → days
directional outcome
Outcome figures are directional placeholders. Validate against a client baseline.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 3 · The solution
28·53
The governed path

With the platform in place, a workload moves through four governed stages.

Each stage has a clear owner and a clear hand-off. Nothing is improvised, and nothing waits on a meeting.
1

Scaffold

Start from a golden-path template with identity and networking pre-wired.

→
2

Build

Standard CI runs tests, evaluations, and policy checks in one pass.

→
3

Deploy

Release to the AKS landing zone with scaling and rollback already defined.

→
4

Operate

Shared observability tracks cost, latency, and model behavior from day one.

Flow note · This is the broken-path slide from Section 2, fixed stage for stage.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 3 · The solution
29·53

Six signals that tell you the platform is actually working.

Headline signal
2wks
median time to provision a new AI workload, down from nine
Time to production
−70%
pilot to live service
Policy rework
−1stage
governance shifted left
Workloads on platform
14
across six teams
Shared observability
100%
one pane, all teams

Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 3 · The solution
30·53
Case study

One client, same shape of problem, measured before and after.

A case study makes the abstract concrete. Problem, approach, result, in three rows, with the headline outcome pulled into the hero panel.
Reference client
Mid-size financial services firm
~600 engineers · existing Azure footprint · 12 AI pilots, 1 in production
9 → 2
weeks to provision a new AI workload, after the platform
Problem
Every pilot rebuilt its own environment and data access. Eleven of twelve never got a production target.
Approach
A six-week foundation phase: AKS landing zone, standard pipeline, and one pilot migrated onto golden paths.
Result
Provisioning dropped from nine weeks to two. Three further workloads reached production within the next quarter.
Anonymization note · Figures and client descriptor are illustrative. Real case studies use cited, approved data.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 3 · The solution
31·53
Common objections

The questions a careful buyer asks, answered directly.

A FAQ slide pre-empts the room's real concerns. Six pairs, two columns, no evasion. If a question needs a longer answer, it gets its own slide.
QDoes this replace our existing Azure setup?
No. The assessment maps the current estate first, and the landing zone is designed around what exists, not on top of a teardown.
QWhat if our teams resist a shared platform?
Two pilot teams co-design the golden paths. The platform reflects real workflows because real teams shaped it.
QHow long until we see value?
The Phase 1 assessment delivers a baseline in three weeks. The first workload runs end to end by week nine of the foundation phase.
QWho owns the platform afterwards?
The client does. Client engineers work inside Phases 2 and 3, so ownership transfers gradually, not in a single hand-off.
QIs this locked to Microsoft tooling?
The reference stack uses Azure services, but the patterns are portable. There is no proprietary lock-in layer.
QWhat does it cost to run, ongoing?
Runtime cost is the AKS and Foundry footprint. The platform itself adds process, not licensing.
FAQ note · These are the recurring objections for this template. Tailor the list to each client's known concerns.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
IV
Section 4 · Architecture
How it fits together
The diagnosis and the reframe are settled. This section is the annotated architecture: real components, real decisions, called out in the open.
Questions this act answers
Q11What are the components, concretely?
Q12How does the pipeline enforce governance?
Q13Build, buy, or reference platform?
Microsoft·Section 4 · Architecture
33·53
Section intro

Where the market is, in one honest read.

A section intro is lighter than a divider. It sets up the sub-topic and frames what the next few slides will show, without the full act ceremony.
What this section covers
Adoption is wide. Production is narrow.
Most enterprises now run AI pilots. Far fewer run AI in production. This section sizes that gap with the client's peers as the reference point, so the problem is concrete before we name its causes.
In this section
4 slides, ending on a client snapshot
Key question
How big is the pilot-to-production gap?
Leaves you with
A measured starting point, not an opinion

Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 4 · Architecture
34·53
Architecture detail

The reference stack, component by component, with the decisions called out.

Where the layer-stack slide is the overview, this is the annotated version: real components in tiers, with the design decisions pulled out as notes.
Experience
Developer portal
Golden-path templates
Service catalog
Self-service provisioning
Delivery
Standard CI
Build, test, evaluate
Policy gates
In-pipeline governance
Release
Promotion with rollback
Runtime & Intelligence
AKS landing zone
Networking, identity, scale
Azure AI Foundry
Model catalog, evaluation
Observability
Cost, latency, behavior
Decided once
Networking and identity live in the landing zone, not per workload.
Shift-left by design
Policy gates run inside CI, so governance is a property of shipping.
One pane
Observability is shared, so every team reads the same signal.
Diagram note · Component names are generic. A real deck names the client's specific services and versions.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 4 · Architecture
35·53
Runtime layer, expanded

The runtime layer is where most decisions get made once.

Zooming into the AKS landing zone: four concerns that every workload would otherwise re-decide, settled at the platform level.
Networking

Virtual network & ingress

One network topology, one ingress pattern. Workloads attach; they do not design.

Identity

Workload identity & secrets

Managed identity and a single secrets pattern. No per-team credential negotiation.

Scaling

Autoscaling & resource policy

Scaling rules and resource quotas defined once, inherited by every namespace.

Isolation

Namespace & policy boundaries

Each team gets a bounded space with guardrails, not a blank cluster.

Architecture note · Component names are generic. Real decks name the client's specific services.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 4 · Architecture
36·53
CI/CD with policy gates

The pipeline is where governance becomes automatic.

Four stages, with policy embedded in stages two and three. Nothing reaches production without passing through them.
1

Commit

Code and config land in a standard repo structure.

→
2

Build & check

Tests, model evaluation, and policy gates run in one pass.

→
3

Promote

Signed artifacts move through environments with rollback defined.

→
4

Observe

Cost, latency, and behavior stream to the shared dashboard.

Flow note · Policy lives in stage 2. That single placement is what shifts governance left.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 4 · Architecture
37·53
Service inventory

Every component, by layer, with its role and owner.

The architecture diagram, written out as a table. This is the reference list a platform team works from.
ServiceLayerRoleOwner
Developer portalExperienceGolden-path templates and self-service provisioning.Platform team
Standard CI/CDDeliveryBuild, evaluate, gate, and promote in one path.Platform team
Policy engineDeliveryIn-pipeline governance rules and enforcement.Security
AKS landing zoneRuntimeNetworking, identity, scaling, isolation.Platform team
Azure AI FoundryIntelligenceModel catalog, evaluation, and versioning.Platform team
Observability stackIntelligenceShared cost, latency, and model-behavior signal.SRE
How to use this

This table is the build checklist. Phase 2 of the roadmap stands up rows one through four; rows five and six follow in Phase 3.

Synthesis line · reference template
Inventory note · Services are generic. Real decks name specific SKUs and versions.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 4 · Architecture
38·53
Build options

Three ways to get a platform. One fits this client.

A vendor-compare slide is an honest feature matrix. The recommended column is highlighted, but every row is filled in for all three options.
Capability Build in-house Reference platform Off-the-shelf PaaS
Time to first workloadMonthsWeeksWeeks
Fits existing Azure estateYesYesPartial
Governance in the pipelineIf builtYesNo
Client owns it at the endYesYesNo
Ongoing vendor lock-inNoneNoneHigh
Internal effort to maintainHighModerateLow
How to read this

In-house gives control at a high cost. Off-the-shelf is fast but rents you the platform. The reference platform is the middle path: built with you, owned by you.

Synthesis line · reference template
Matrix note · Ratings are qualitative for the template. Real comparisons cite specifics and are reviewed with the account team.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 4 · Architecture
39·53
Weighted decision

The build-versus-platform call, scored on the criteria that matter.

A decision matrix shows the reasoning, not just the conclusion. Each option is scored against weighted criteria, and the totals are computed in the open.
Criterion Weight Build in-house Reference platform Off-the-shelf PaaS
Speed to production30%1.22.72.4
Fit with current estate25%2.32.51.0
Long-term ownership25%2.52.50.8
Internal effort to sustain20%0.81.82.4
Weighted total100%1.672.411.62
What the score says

The reference platform wins not on any single row, but on balance: it is the only option that scores well on speed, fit, and ownership at once.

Scoring is illustrative · reference template
Scoring note · Weights and scores are placeholders. Set them with the client so the decision is theirs.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
V
Section 5 · The plan
From decision to a platform teams build on
The architecture is settled. What remains is a staged path, a clear team, and an honest view of who owns what.
Questions this act answers
Q14What is the staged path and timeline?
Q15Who runs the engagement?
Q16Who owns the platform afterward?
Microsoft·Section 5 · The plan
41·53
Modernization roadmap

A staged path from assessment to a platform other teams build on.

Each phase ends with something usable. No phase depends on the next one being perfect. Timeframes are indicative.
Phase 1

Assess

Weeks 1–3
  • Map current AI workloads and blockers
  • Baseline delivery and cost metrics
  • Agree target platform scope
Exit criteria

Signed-off scope document and a measured delivery baseline.

Phase 2

Foundation

Weeks 4–9
  • Stand up the AKS landing zone
  • Establish identity and networking
  • Wire the standard CI/CD path
Exit criteria

One workload running through the standard pipeline end to end.

Phase 3

Golden paths

Weeks 10–16
  • Publish self-service templates
  • Onboard two pilot teams
  • Embed policy gates and observability
Exit criteria

Two pilot teams shipping from golden-path templates.

Phase 4

Scale

Weeks 17+
  • Roll out to remaining teams
  • Hand platform ownership to client
  • Establish continuous improvement loop
Exit criteria

Platform owned by the client, with an active improvement loop.

Timeframes assume a mid-size org with an existing Azure footprint. Re-scope per engagement.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 5 · The plan
42·53
Detailed timeline

Twelve weeks, four phases, with the overlaps shown honestly.

A gantt makes dependencies visible. Phase 3 starts before Phase 2 fully closes, on purpose: golden paths are designed while the foundation settles.
WorkstreamW1W2W3W4W5W6W7W8W9W10W11W12
Phase 1 · Assess
Weeks 1–3
Phase 2 · Foundation
Weeks 4–9
Phase 3 · Golden paths
Weeks 7–10, overlaps
Phase 4 · Scale
Weeks 10–12+
Timeline note · Weeks assume a mid-size org with an existing Azure footprint. Re-scope per engagement.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 5 · The plan
43·53
The engagement team

Four roles, clear ownership, no ambiguity about who decides what.

A small team by design. Each person owns one thing, and two of the four seats are filled by the client so the hand-over starts on day one.
PS

Frontier Cockpit Team

Engagement lead

Software Global Black Belt. Owns the technical direction and the client relationship end to end.

AE

Account exec

Commercial owner

Holds scope, timeline, and the commercial conversation. Single point for contracting.

PE

Platform engineer

Build lead

Stands up the landing zone and the standard pipeline. Pairs with client engineers.

CE

Client engineer

Embedded

Two client engineers work inside the engagement, so ownership transfers as the platform is built.

Staffing note · Role names are generic for the template. A real deck names individuals and their tenure.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 5 · The plan
44·53
Ownership matrix

Who is responsible, accountable, consulted, informed for each workstream.

A RACI matrix removes the most common engagement failure: ambiguity about who owns what. Each cell is one letter, and ownership shifts to the client down the rows.
Workstream Client lead MS engagement Platform team Client ops
Platform scope & designACRI
Landing zone buildIRAC
CI/CD pipeline setupIRAC
Policy & governance rulesCCAR
Pilot workload migrationCARC
Platform ownership, ongoingAICR
R Responsible A Accountable C Consulted I Informed
RACI note · One accountable party per row. Calibrate the grid with the client during the assessment.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 5 · The plan
45·53
Engagement risks

The honest version: what could go wrong, and how each is handled.

A proposal without a risk slide is a sales pitch. Each row pairs a real risk with a concrete mitigation already in the plan.
Risk Likelihood Impact Mitigation
Teams resist a shared platform Medium High Two pilot teams co-design the golden paths in Phase 3, so the platform reflects real workflows.
Scope expands mid-engagement High Medium Phase 1 ends with a signed scope document. Changes are explicit re-scopes, not silent creep.
Existing Azure footprint conflicts Medium Medium The assessment maps the current estate first. The landing zone is designed around what exists.
Hand-over leaves a knowledge gap Medium High Client engineers work inside Phases 2 and 3, not just at the end. Ownership transfers gradually.
Platform value is hard to prove Watch Medium The Phase 1 baseline is the comparison point. Later metrics read against the client's own start line.
Why this slide exists

Naming risks early is what makes the rest of the proposal credible. Every mitigation here is already a line in the roadmap.

Synthesis line · reference template
Banding note · Likelihood and impact are qualitative. Calibrate with the client during the assessment.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
VI
Section 6 · Investment
What it takes, and what it returns
The plan is set. This section is the investment shape: three engagement sizes, the specific ask, and the recommendation.
Questions this act answers
Q16What does each engagement size include?
Q17What is the specific first ask?
Microsoft·Section 6 · Investment
47·53
Investment shape

Three engagement sizes. Each ends with something usable, none locks in the next.

Figures are placeholders. The point is the shape: a small committed first step, a foundation phase, and a full rollout chosen on evidence.
Tier 1 · Assess
3 wks · fixed
Low commitment, high clarity
  • Workload and blocker inventory
  • Delivery and cost baseline
  • Prioritized platform scope
  • Go or no-go recommendation
Ends with the client's own data and a clear decision point.
Most common start
Tier 2 · Foundation
6 wks · scoped
The platform baseline, stood up
  • AKS landing zone live
  • Identity and networking established
  • Standard CI/CD path wired
  • One workload running end to end
Ends with a working platform and one production workload on it.
Tier 3 · Scale
Ongoing · phased
Rollout and hand-over
  • Golden-path templates published
  • Remaining teams onboarded
  • Ownership transferred to client
  • Continuous improvement loop
Ends with the platform owned and run by the client team.
Pricing note · Durations are illustrative. Real proposals carry costed figures reviewed with the account team.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Microsoft·Section 6 · Investment
48·53
Recommendation

Start with a three-week assessment. Decide the platform scope on evidence, not on this deck.

The assessment is low-commitment and produces the client's own baseline. Everything after it is a decision the client makes with real numbers.
01

Immediate · the assessment

Three weeks, fixed scope. Produces a baseline of delivery metrics and a prioritized blocker list.

02

Near-term · the foundation

Stand up the landing zone and standard pipeline. First pilot team onboarded by week nine.

03

Sustained · the handover

Platform ownership transfers to the client team with golden paths and an improvement loop in place.

The ask
3 wks
to a decision backed by the client's own data, not assumptions.
Next step

Confirm an assessment window and the two candidate workloads to baseline.

Contact · frontier-cockpit@example.com
This is a reference template. Structure, palette, and component set are the deliverable; the content is illustrative.
Microsoft · Strategic Brief
Appendix
Reference material
Supporting detail for the brief: the full assessment checklist, the service inventory, and the data behind each illustrative figure. Everything past this point is reference, not narrative.
Assessment checklist Service inventory Figure sources Glossary
Microsoft·AI Platform Modernization
The platform decision behind every production AI outcome.
A strategic and technical brief on why enterprise AI initiatives stall before production, and the reference architecture that closes the gap between pilot and scale.
Azure AI Foundry GitHub Copilot Enterprise Azure Kubernetes Service Agentic DevOps
Frontier Cockpit TeamSoftware Global Black Belt
Strategic & Technical BriefClient engagement · 2026
Confidential · Client brief 50 / 53
The opening number
3×
longer time-to-production for AI workloads built on ad hoc infrastructure versus an opinionated platform baseline.
Illustrative figure for the reference template. Replace with cited industry data in real decks.
I.ContextWhere the market is
II.SolutionPlatform as the unit of delivery
III.ExecuteRoadmap and investment
Microsoft·AI Platform Modernization
The platform decision behind every production AI outcome.
A strategic and technical brief on why enterprise AI initiatives stall before production, and the reference architecture that closes the gap between pilot and scale.
Azure AI Foundry GitHub Copilot Enterprise Azure Kubernetes Service Agentic DevOps
Frontier Cockpit TeamSoftware Global Black Belt
Strategic & Technical BriefClient engagement · 2026
Confidential · Client brief 51 / 53
The thesis
The model is not the bottleneck. The platform underneath it is what decides whether a pilot ever becomes a service the business can depend on.
This brief makes the case for treating the platform as the unit of delivery, then lays out the architecture, roadmap, and investment to build one. Content is illustrative for the reference template.
Microsoft·AI Platform Modernization
The platform decision behind every production AI outcome.
A strategic and technical brief on why enterprise AI initiatives stall before production, and the reference architecture that closes the gap between pilot and scale.
Azure AI Foundry GitHub Copilot Enterprise Azure Kubernetes Service Agentic DevOps
Frontier Cockpit TeamSoftware Global Black Belt
Strategic & Technical BriefClient engagement · 2026
Confidential · Client brief 52 / 53
Three horizons
The brief is built on three moves, each earning the next.
I

Diagnose the gap

Why pilots stall before production, sized with the client's own data.

II

Reframe the solution

Platform as the unit of delivery, with an opinionated reference stack.

III

Execute the plan

A staged roadmap, a clear investment, and an honest view of the risks.

Next step

Confirm an assessment window, and we begin.

The three-week assessment is low-commitment and produces the client's own baseline. Everything after it is a decision made with real numbers.

What we need from you
A three-week window and the two candidate workloads to baseline.
What you get back
A measured baseline and a prioritized platform scope.
Frontier Cockpit Team
Software Global Black Belt
frontier-cockpit@example.com
